items.front: Under 2-4-1, what is the prohibition when combining positions in a terminal tower?
items.back: Do not use ground control frequency for airborne communications.

items.front: List the four interphone transmission priorities in order under 2-4-10.
items.back: 1st: Emergency messages (including accident information). 2nd: Clearances and control instructions. 3rd: Movement and control messages (progress reports > departure/arrival reports > flight plans). 4th: Movement messages on VFR aircraft.

items.front: What two words are used to interrupt lower-priority interphone messages under 2-4-11?
Under 2-4-14, what is the rule for Air Force One/Two and the heavy designator?
items.back: Do NOT add the heavy designator to the call sign. State only 'Air Force One' or 'Air Force Two' regardless of aircraft type.

items.front: Under 2-4-14, list the four exceptions when en route controllers must use super/heavy.
items.back: 1. Communications with a terminal facility about super/heavy operations. 2. En route center providing approach control service. 3. Separation from following aircraft may become less than 5 miles. 4. When issuing traffic advisories.

items.front: Under 2-4-15, how are similar sounding call signs handled?
items.back: Restate the call sign after the flight number. Example: 'United Thirty-one United.' Also: do not abbreviate similar sounding IDs, and notify each pilot of the other aircraft on frequency.

items.front: Where are the ICAO phonetics and pronunciation found under Chapter 2?
items.back: TBL 2-4-1. Controllers must use ICAO pronunciation of numbers and individual letters.

items.front: Under 2-4-17, how are serial numbers pronounced?
items.back: The separate digits. Example: 11,495 = 'One one four niner five.'

items.front: Under 2-4-17, how are radar beacon codes stated?
items.back: The separate digits of the 4-digit code. Example: 2100 = 'Two one zero zero.'

items.front: Under 2-4-17, how is the number 0 pronounced, and when is it not?
items.back: As 'zero' except when used in approved group form for authorized aircraft call signs and in stating altitudes. Example: 'Western five thirty' (group form).

items.front: Under 2-4-19, how are approach control facilities (RAPCON/RATCF/ARAC) identified?
items.back: Facility name followed by 'approach.' If military and civil share a location with similar names, military service name is added: 'Navy Jacksonville Approach.'

items.front: Under 2-4-22, how are airspace classes pronounced?
items.back: In ICAO phonetics: Alfa, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Golf. The term 'Class' may be dropped in pilot/controller communications.

items.front: Under 2-4-3, what three things must a controller ensure during a pilot's read back?
items.back: 1. Items read back are correct. 2. Hold short instructions are read back (taxi or LAHSO). 3. Pilot used call sign and/or registration number in the read back.

items.front: Under 2-4-6, what four actions are required when false or deceptive communications occur?
items.back: 1. Correct false information. 2. Broadcast an alert to all aircraft on all frequencies in the affected area. 3. Collect pertinent information. 4. Notify the operations supervisor.

items.front: Under 2-4-8, what is the sequence for initial radio contact from a sector/position?
items.back: 1. Identification of aircraft. 2. Identification of ATC unit. 3. Message (if any). 4. The word 'over' if required.

items.front: Under 2-4-9, when may aircraft identifications be abbreviated and when must they NOT be?
items.back: MAY abbreviate: U.S. civil aircraft using the prefix and last 3 digits/letters after communications established. MUST NOT abbreviate: similar sounding IDs, ICAO 3LD call signs, FAA authorized call signs, military call signs.

items.front: Under 2-4-12, what is the interphone message closure sequence?
items.back: Receiver states response + operating initials. Then caller states operating initials. Both parties must include initials to terminate the exchange.

items.front: Under 2-4-5, what types of messages may controllers transmit?
items.back: Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.

items.front: Under 2-4-7, what are the FAA Administrator and Deputy Administrator air-to-ground call signs?
